articles.title: Manx Wildlife Week: Fun Events to Explore Nature
articles.summary: The Isle of Man is celebrating its beautiful nature with a special week called Manx Wildlife Week. There are over 25 events like beach clean-ups, bird watching, nature walks, and workshops. People of all ages can join and learn about animals, plants, and the environment. The events are free and fun for kids and adults. You can go on a bee trail, watch birds, or do a wildlife obstacle course. This week helps everyone connect with nature and have a good time outdoors.

articles.title: A Giant Octopus That Lived with Dinosaurs
articles.summary: A long time ago, about 80 million years, the oceans were home to huge creatures like mosasaurs and plesiosaurs. Scientists thought the biggest hunters were animals with backbones. But a new study found something amazing: a giant octopus that could be up to 19 meters long! Octopuses are soft and rarely leave fossils, but scientists discovered their hard beaks in rocks. They used a special method to grind rocks layer by layer and then used a computer to find the beaks. The beaks were big and had scratches, showing the octopus was a strong hunter. This changes what we know about who was at the top of the food chain back then.

articles.title: A New Force in Space?
articles.summary: Scientists learn by testing new ideas. Sometimes, the universe acts differently in different places. Far away, the universe is expanding in a way that doesn't match our current understanding. But in our solar system, everything seems normal. A NASA scientist named Slava Turyshev has an idea. He thinks there might be a 'fifth force' hiding in our solar system. This force would be different from gravity, electromagnetism, and the two nuclear forces. It might be weak and hard to detect because of something called 'screening.' In places with a lot of matter, like near the Sun, this force becomes very weak. In empty space, it becomes stronger. Scientists need to design very careful experiments to find this force. If they succeed, it could help explain dark energy and dark matter.

articles.title: Astronomers Find a New Type of Space Explosion
articles.summary: When big stars die, they explode and send elements like carbon and iron into space. Sometimes, two dead stars called neutron stars crash into each other, creating a kilonova explosion that makes gold and uranium. Scientists have only seen one clear kilonova, in 2017. Now, they might have found a second one, but it's different. The new event, called AT2025ulz, started like a kilonova but then looked like a supernova. Some scientists think it might be a "superkilonova" – a kilonova caused by a supernova. This has never been seen before. The discovery could help us learn more about how elements are made in space.

articles.title: Where Do Tiny Plastic Bits in the Air Come From?
articles.summary: Scientists wanted to know where tiny pieces of plastic in the air come from. These tiny plastics are called microplastics. They are so small you can't see them. They can float in the air and travel far away. People and animals can breathe them in. This might be bad for health. A new study from the University of Vienna used computers and measurements to find the answer. They looked at how much plastic comes from land and how much from the ocean. The scientists found that more than 20 times more tiny plastics come from land than from the ocean. Land sources include car tires and clothes. The ocean also releases some, but much less. This helps us understand where the pollution comes from. We need more studies to learn more.

articles.title: Ice Secrets: How Scientists Learned About Earth's Past Climate
articles.summary: Scientists study ancient ice in Antarctica to learn about Earth's climate from long ago. The ice has tiny air bubbles inside that hold old air. By looking at this ice, scientists can tell what the weather was like millions of years ago. They found that Earth was much warmer 3 million years ago. There were forests in places like Alaska and Greenland that are cold today. Sea levels were also higher. Over time, Earth cooled down. But a new study shows that the amount of greenhouse gases in the air did not drop much. This is surprising because greenhouse gases trap heat. Scientists are still trying to understand why Earth cooled even though greenhouse gases stayed almost the same. They also learned that the ocean cooled by about 2 degrees Celsius. This research helps us understand how climate changes over very long periods.
